title: Finding the Jacobian
description: Given a transformation, find its Jacobian.

assessment_items.question_content:
$f(x, y, z) = \begin{bmatrix}
x - z \\ \\
\sin(y) \\ \\
z - x
\end{bmatrix}$

**Find the remaining entries in the Jacobian matrix of $f$.**

[[☃ label-image 1]]
hints:
The Jacobian matrix of a transformation $f$ holds all of its partial derivative information. Each row of the matrix is the gradient of one of the components.

Suppose $f(x, y, z) = \begin{bmatrix}f_0 \\ \\ f_1 \\ \\ f_2 \end{bmatrix}$.

Then its Jacobian is below.

$J(f) = \begin{bmatrix}
\dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial z} \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial y} &  \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial z}\\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial z}
\end{bmatrix}$

The missing entries in the Jacobian matrix that we wish to compute are:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial x} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x} \left[ 
x - z \right] = 1 \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x} \left[ 
\sin(y) \right] = 0 \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial z} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial z} \left[ 
z - x \right] = 1
\end{align}$

Therefore:

$J(f) = \begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & -1 \\ \\
0 & \cos(y) & 0 \\ \\
-1 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}$

options.imageAlt: A 3 by 3 square matrix is labeled as J of f. The first row comprises 1, 0, and negative. The second row has 0, cos (y), and 0. The third row -1, 0, and 1.

assessment_items.question_content:
$f(x, y, z) = \begin{bmatrix}
ye^z \\ \\
x - y \\ \\
3y + z^2
\end{bmatrix}$

**Find the remaining entries in the Jacobian matrix of $f$.**

[[☃ label-image 1]]
hints:
The Jacobian matrix of a transformation $f$ holds all of its partial derivative information. Each row of the matrix is the gradient of one of the components.

Suppose $f(x, y, z) = \begin{bmatrix}f_0 \\ \\ f_1 \\ \\ f_2 \end{bmatrix}$.

Then its Jacobian is below.

$J(f) = \begin{bmatrix}
\dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial z} \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial y} &  \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial z}\\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial z}
\end{bmatrix}$

The missing entries in the Jacobian matrix that we wish to compute are:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial x} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x} \left[ 
ye^z \right] = 0 \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x} \left[ 
x - y \right] = 1 \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial z} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial z} \left[ 
3z + z^2 \right] = 2z
\end{align}$

Therefore:

$J(f) = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & e^z & ye^z \\ \\
1 & -1 & 0 \\ \\
0 & 3 & 2z
\end{bmatrix}$

options.imageAlt: A 3 by 3 matrix is shown. Row 1: Blank, e to the power of z, y times e to the power of z. Row 2: blank, negative 1, and 0. Row 3: 0, 3, and blank.

assessment_items.question_content:
$f(x, y, z) = \begin{bmatrix}
y\sin(z) \\ \\
x^3 - xz \\ \\
3 + xy
\end{bmatrix}$

**Find the remaining entries in the Jacobian matrix of $f$.**

[[☃ label-image 1]]
hints:
The Jacobian matrix of a transformation $f$ holds all of its partial derivative information. Each row of the matrix is the gradient of one of the components.

Suppose $f(x, y, z) = \begin{bmatrix}f_0 \\ \\ f_1 \\ \\ f_2 \end{bmatrix}$.

Then its Jacobian is below.

$J(f) = \begin{bmatrix}
\dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial z} \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial y} &  \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial z}\\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial z}
\end{bmatrix}$

The missing entries in the Jacobian matrix that we wish to compute are:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial z} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial z} \left[ 
y\sin(z) \right] = y\cos(z) \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x} \left[ 
x^3 - xz \right] = 3x^2 - z \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial y} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y} \left[ 3 + xy \right] = x
\end{align}$

Therefore:

$J(f) = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & \sin(z) & y\cos(z) \\ \\
3x^2 - z & 0 & -x \\ \\
y & x & 0
\end{bmatrix}$

options.imageAlt: A 3 by 3 matrix is shown. Row 1: 0, sine of z, and blank. Row 2: blank, 0, and negative z. Row 3: y, blank, and 0.

assessment_items.question_content:
$f(x, y, z) = \begin{bmatrix}
e^xe^y \\ \\
y - x^2 \\ \\
-x^2 + z
\end{bmatrix}$

**Find the remaining entries in the Jacobian matrix of $f$.**

[[☃ label-image 1]]
hints:
The Jacobian matrix of a transformation $f$ holds all of its partial derivative information. Each row of the matrix is the gradient of one of the components.

Suppose $f(x, y, z) = \begin{bmatrix}f_0 \\ \\ f_1 \\ \\ f_2 \end{bmatrix}$.

Then its Jacobian is below.

$J(f) = \begin{bmatrix}
\dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial z} \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial y} &  \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial z}\\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial z}
\end{bmatrix}$

The missing entries in the Jacobian matrix that we wish to compute are:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial y} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y} \left[ 
e^xe^y \right] = e^xe^y \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x} \left[ 
y - x^2 \right] = -2x \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial z} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial z} \left[ 
-x^2 + z \right] = 1
\end{align}$

Therefore:

$J(f) = \begin{bmatrix}
e^xe^y & e^xe^y & 0 \\ \\
-2x & 1 & 0 \\ \\
-2x & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}$

options.imageAlt: A 3 by 3 matrix is shown. Row 1: e to the power of x times e to the power of y, blank, and 0. Row 2: blank, 1, and 0. Row 3: Negative 2 times x, 0, and blank.

assessment_items.question_content:
$f(x, y, z) = \begin{bmatrix}
z^{-1} \\ \\
x^2 + y^2 \\ \\
2 - y
\end{bmatrix}$

**Find the remaining entries in the Jacobian matrix of $f$.**

[[☃ label-image 1]]
hints:
The Jacobian matrix of a transformation $f$ holds all of its partial derivative information. Each row of the matrix is the gradient of one of the components.

Suppose $f(x, y, z) = \begin{bmatrix}f_0 \\ \\ f_1 \\ \\ f_2 \end{bmatrix}$.

Then its Jacobian is below.

$J(f) = \begin{bmatrix}
\dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial z} \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial y} &  \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial z}\\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial z}
\end{bmatrix}$

The missing entries in the Jacobian matrix that we wish to compute are:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial z} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial z} \left[ 
z^{-1} \right] = -z^{-2} \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial y} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x} \left[ 
x^2 + y^2 \right] = 2y \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial z} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial z} \left[ 
x^2 + y^2 \right] = 0
\end{align}$

Therefore:

$J(f) = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 0 & -z^{-2} \\ \\
2x & 2y & 0 \\ \\
0 & -1 & 0
\end{bmatrix}$

options.imageAlt: A 3 by 3 matrix is shown. Row 1: 0, 0, and blank. Row 2: 2 times x, blank, and blank. Row 3: 0, negative 1, and 0.

assessment_items.question_content:
$f(x, y, z) = \begin{bmatrix}
y + z \\ \\
\cos(yz) \\ \\
x^2 - 1
\end{bmatrix}$

**Find the remaining entries in the Jacobian matrix of $f$.**

[[☃ label-image 1]]
hints:
The Jacobian matrix of a transformation $f$ holds all of its partial derivative information. Each row of the matrix is the gradient of one of the components.

Suppose $f(x, y, z) = \begin{bmatrix}f_0 \\ \\ f_1 \\ \\ f_2 \end{bmatrix}$.

Then its Jacobian is below.

$J(f) = \begin{bmatrix}
\dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial z} \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial y} &  \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial z}\\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial z}
\end{bmatrix}$

The missing entries in the Jacobian matrix that we wish to compute are:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial y} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y} \left[ 
y + z \right] = 1 \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial z} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial z} \left[ 
y + z \right] = 1 \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial y} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y} \left[ 
\cos(yz) \right] = -z\sin(yz)
\end{align}$

Therefore:

$J(f) = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 1 & 1 \\ \\
0 & -z\sin(yz) & -y\sin(yz) \\ \\
2x & 0 & 0
\end{bmatrix}$

options.imageAlt: A 3 by 3 matrix is shown. Row 1: 0, blank, and blank. Row 2: 0, blank, and negative y times sine of y times z. Row 3: 2 times x, 0, and 0.

assessment_items.question_content:
$f(u, v) = \begin{bmatrix}
\cos(u)\cos(v) \\ \\
\sin(u)\cos(v) \\ \\
\sin(v)
\end{bmatrix}$

**Find the remaining entries in the Jacobian matrix of $f$.**

[[☃ label-image 1]]
hints:
The Jacobian matrix of a transformation $f$ holds all of its partial derivative information. Each row of the matrix is the gradient of one of the components.

Suppose $f(u, v) = \begin{bmatrix}f_0 \\ \\ f_1 \\ \\ f_2 \end{bmatrix}$.

Then its Jacobian is below.

$J(f) = \begin{bmatrix}
\dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial u} & \dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial v} \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial u} & \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial v} \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial u} & \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial v}
\end{bmatrix}$

The missing entries in the Jacobian matrix that we wish to compute are:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial v} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial v} \left[ 
\cos(u)\cos(v) \right] = -\cos(u)\sin(v) \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial u} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial u} \left[ 
\sin(u)\cos(v) \right] = \cos(u)\cos(v) \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial u} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial u} \left[ 
\sin(v) \right] = 0
\end{align}$

Therefore:

$J(f) = \begin{bmatrix}
-\sin(u)\cos(v) & -\cos(u)\sin(v) \\ \\
\cos(u)\cos(v) & -\sin(u)\sin(v) \\ \\
0 & \cos(v)
\end{bmatrix}$

options.imageAlt: A 3 by 3 matrix is shown. Row 1: negative sine of u times cosine of v, blank, and blank. Row 2: blank, blank, and negative sine of u times sine of v, blank. Row 3: blank, blank, and cosine of v.

assessment_items.question_content:
$f(x, y) = \begin{bmatrix}
y - x^2 \\ \\
2x + y^2x \\ \\
x^3 + y - y^2
\end{bmatrix}$

**Find the remaining entries in the Jacobian matrix of $f$.**

[[☃ label-image 1]]
hints:
The Jacobian matrix of a transformation $f$ holds all of its partial derivative information. Each row of the matrix is the gradient of one of the components.

Suppose $f(x, y) = \begin{bmatrix}f_0 \\ \\ f_1 \\ \\ f_2 \end{bmatrix}$.

Then its Jacobian is below.

$J(f) = \begin{bmatrix}
\dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial y} \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial y} \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial y}
\end{bmatrix}$

The missing entries in the Jacobian matrix that we wish to compute are:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial x} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x} \left[ 
y - x^2 \right] = -2x \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial y} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y} \left[ 
2x + y^2x \right] = 2xy \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial y} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y} \left[ 
x^3 + y - y^2 \right] = 1 - 2y
\end{align}$

Therefore:

$J(f) = \begin{bmatrix}
-2x & 1 \\ \\
2 + y^2 & 2xy \\ \\
3x^2 & 1 - 2y
\end{bmatrix}$

options.imageAlt: A 3 by 3 matrix is shown. Row 1: blank, blank, and 1. Row 2: 2 plus y squared, blank, and blank. Row 3: 3 x squared, blank, and blank.

assessment_items.question_content:
$f(x, y) = \begin{bmatrix}
\sin(y) \\ \\
x - 4y \\ \\
xy^2
\end{bmatrix}$

**Find the remaining entries in the Jacobian matrix of $f$.**

[[☃ label-image 1]]
hints:
The Jacobian matrix of a transformation $f$ holds all of its partial derivative information. Each row of the matrix is the gradient of one of the components.

Suppose $f(x, y) = \begin{bmatrix}f_0 \\ \\ f_1 \\ \\ f_2 \end{bmatrix}$.

Then its Jacobian is below.

$J(f) = \begin{bmatrix}
\dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial y} \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial y} \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial y}
\end{bmatrix}$

The missing entries in the Jacobian matrix that we wish to compute are:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial y} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y} \left[ 
\sin(y) \right] = \cos(y) \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial y} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y} \left[ 
x - 4y \right] = -4 \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x} \left[ 
xy^2 \right] = y^2
\end{align}$

Therefore:

$J(f) = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & \cos(y) \\ \\
1 & -4 \\ \\
y^2 & 2xy
\end{bmatrix}$

options.imageAlt: A 3 by 3 matrix is shown. Row 1: 0, blank, and blank. Row 2: 1, blank, and blank. Row 3: blank, blank, and 2 times x y.

assessment_items.question_content:
$f(x, y) = \begin{bmatrix}
3xy^4 \\ \\
y - 4 \\ \\
\cos(x^2)
\end{bmatrix}$

**Find the remaining entries in the Jacobian matrix of $f$.**

[[☃ label-image 1]]
hints:
The Jacobian matrix of a transformation $f$ holds all of its partial derivative information. Each row of the matrix is the gradient of one of the components.

Suppose $f(x, y) = \begin{bmatrix}f_0 \\ \\ f_1 \\ \\ f_2 \end{bmatrix}$.

Then its Jacobian is below.

$J(f) = \begin{bmatrix}
\dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial y} \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial y} \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial y}
\end{bmatrix}$

The missing entries in the Jacobian matrix that we wish to compute are:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial x} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x} \left[ 
3xy^4 \right] = 4y^4 \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x} \left[ 
\cos(x^2) \right] = -2x\sin(x^2) \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial y} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y} \left[ \cos(x^2) \right] = 0
\end{align}$

Therefore:

$J(f) = \begin{bmatrix}
3y^4 & 12xy^3 \\ \\
0 & 1 \\ \\
-2x\sin(x^2) & 0
\end{bmatrix}$

options.imageAlt: A 3 by 3 matrix is shown. Row 1: blank, blank, and 12 x y cubed. Row 2: 0, blank, and 1. Row 3: blank, blank, and blank.

assessment_items.question_content:
$f(x, y) = \begin{bmatrix}
\sin(y) \\ \\
x^3 + \cos(x) \\ \\
x^2y^2
\end{bmatrix}$

**Find the remaining entries in the Jacobian matrix of $f$.**

[[☃ label-image 1]]
hints:
The Jacobian matrix of a transformation $f$ holds all of its partial derivative information. Each row of the matrix is the gradient of one of the components.

Suppose $f(x, y) = \begin{bmatrix}f_0 \\ \\ f_1 \\ \\ f_2 \end{bmatrix}$.

Then its Jacobian is below.

$J(f) = \begin{bmatrix}
\dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial y} \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial y} \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial y}
\end{bmatrix}$

The missing entries in the Jacobian matrix that we wish to compute are:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial x} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x} \left[ 
\sin(y) \right] = 0 \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial y} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y} \left[ 
\sin(y) \right] = \cos(y) \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x} \left[ x^2y^2 \right] = 2xy^2
\end{align}$

Therefore:

$J(f) = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & \cos(y) \\ \\
3x^2 - \sin(x) & 0 \\ \\
2xy^2 & 2x^2y
\end{bmatrix}$

options.imageAlt: A 3 by 3 matrix is shown. Row 1: blank, blank, and blank. Row 2: 3 x squared minus sine of x, blank, and 0. Row 3: blank, blank, and 2 x squared y.

assessment_items.question_content:
$f(x, y, z) = \begin{bmatrix}
xz - \sin(y) \\ \\
y^2 + x
\end{bmatrix}$

**Find the remaining entries in the Jacobian matrix of $f$.**

[[☃ label-image 1]]
hints:
The Jacobian matrix of a transformation $f$ holds all of its partial derivative information. Each row of the matrix is the gradient of one of the components.

Suppose $f(x, y, z) = \begin{bmatrix}f_0 \\ \\ f_1\end{bmatrix}$.

Then its Jacobian is below.

$J(f) = \begin{bmatrix}
\dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial z}  \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial z} 
\end{bmatrix}$

The missing entries in the Jacobian matrix that we wish to compute are:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial y} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y} \left[ 
xz - \sin(y) \right] = -\cos(y) \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial z} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial z} \left[ 
xz - \sin(y) \right] = x \\ \\
\dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x} \left[ y^2 + x \right] = 1
\end{align}$

Therefore:

$J(f) = \begin{bmatrix}
z & -\cos(y) & x \\ \\
1 & 2y & 0
\end{bmatrix}$

options.imageAlt: A 3 by 3 matrix is shown. Row 1: z, blank, and blank. Row 2: blank, blank, and blank. Row 3: blank, 2 y, and 0.
